   148→        symm_mem.set_signal_pad_size(custom_size)
   149→
   150→        # Allocate symmetric memory and verify the signal pad size
   151→        t = symm_mem.empty(64, device="cuda")
   152→        symm_mem_hdl = symm_mem.rendezvous(t, group=dist.group.WORLD)
   153→
   154→        # Verify the allocated symmetric memory uses the custom signal pad size
   155→        self.assertEqual(symm_mem_hdl.signal_pad_size, custom_size)
   156→
   157→        # Test that signal pad operations work with the custom size
   158→        signal_pad = symm_mem_hdl.get_signal_pad(self.rank)
   159→        expected_numel = custom_size // 4  # uint32_t
   160→        self.assertEqual(signal_pad.numel(), expected_numel)
   161→
   162→        # Verify we can use the full custom signal pad
   163→        signal_pad.fill_(0)
   164→        signal_pad[0] = 42
   165→        self.assertEqual(signal_pad[0].item(), 42)
   166→
   167→        # Restore original settings
   168→        symm_mem.set_signal_pad_size(original_size)
   169→
   170→    @skipIf(
   171→        not PLATFORM_SUPPORTS_SYMM_MEM, "SymmMem is not supported on this ROCm arch"
   172→    )
   173→    def test_large_alloc(self) -> None:
   174→        t = symm_mem.empty(2 * 1024**3, dtype=torch.uint8, device="cuda")
   175→        self.assertEqual(t.numel() * t.element_size(), 2 * 1024**3)

   177→    @skipIf(

   179→    )
   180→    @skip_if_lt_x_gpu(2)
   181→    def test_get_signal_pad(self) -> None:
   182→        self._init_process()
   183→
   184→        t = symm_mem.empty(1, device="cuda")
   185→        symm_mem_hdl = symm_mem.rendezvous(t, group=dist.group.WORLD)
   186→        peer_rank = (self.rank + 1) % self.world_size
   187→
   188→        signal_pad = symm_mem_hdl.get_signal_pad(self.rank)
   189→        self.assertEqual(
   190→            signal_pad.data_ptr(), symm_mem_hdl.signal_pad_ptrs[symm_mem_hdl.rank]
   191→        )
   192→
   193→        signal_pad = symm_mem_hdl.get_signal_pad(peer_rank)
   194→        self.assertEqual(signal_pad.dtype, torch.uint32)
   195→        self.assertEqual(signal_pad.numel(), symm_mem_hdl.signal_pad_size // 4)
   196→
   197→        # Only specify sizes
   198→        signal_pad = symm_mem_hdl.get_signal_pad(peer_rank, (8, 8))
   199→        self.assertEqual(signal_pad.dtype, torch.uint32)
   200→        self.assertEqual(signal_pad.numel(), 64)
   201→
   202→        # Only specify dtype
   203→        signal_pad = symm_mem_hdl.get_signal_pad(peer_rank, dtype=torch.uint64)
   204→        self.assertEqual(signal_pad.dtype, torch.uint64)
   205→        self.assertEqual(signal_pad.numel(), symm_mem_hdl.signal_pad_size // 8)
   206→
   207→        # Specify both sizes and dtype
   208→        signal_pad = symm_mem_hdl.get_signal_pad(peer_rank, (8, 8), dtype=torch.uint64)
   209→        self.assertEqual(signal_pad.dtype, torch.uint64)
   210→        self.assertEqual(signal_pad.numel(), 64)
   211→
   212→        # Sanity check that writes to buffer doesn't corrupt signal_pad
   213→        t = symm_mem.empty(1, device="cuda")
   214→        symm_mem_hdl = symm_mem.rendezvous(t, group=dist.group.WORLD)
   215→        signal_pad = symm_mem_hdl.get_signal_pad(self.rank)
   216→        signal_pad.fill_(42)
   217→        t.fill_(0)
   218→        self.assertTrue(signal_pad.eq(42).all())

   259→

   309→    @skip_if_lt_x_gpu(4)
   310→    def test_subgroup(self) -> None:
   311→        self._init_process()
   312→
   313→        ranks = list(range(self.world_size))
   314→        subgroup_0 = dist.new_group(ranks[: len(ranks) // 2])
   315→        subgroup_1 = dist.new_group(ranks[len(ranks) // 2 :])
   316→
   317→        world = dist.group.WORLD
   318→        subgroup = subgroup_0 if world.rank() < world.size() // 2 else subgroup_1
   319→
   320→        t = symm_mem.empty(64, device="cuda")
   321→        symm_mem_world = symm_mem.rendezvous(t, group=world)
   322→        symm_mem_subgroup = symm_mem.rendezvous(t, group=subgroup)
   323→
   324→        self.assertEqual(symm_mem_world.world_size, world.size())
   325→        self.assertEqual(symm_mem_world.rank, world.rank())
   326→        self.assertEqual(symm_mem_subgroup.world_size, world.size() // 2)
   327→        self.assertEqual(symm_mem_subgroup.rank, world.rank() % subgroup.size())
   328→
   329→        t.fill_(world.rank())
   330→        symm_mem_world.barrier()
   331→
   332→        # Observe a peer buffer via the world group
   333→        peer_rank = (world.rank() + 1) % world.size()
   334→        buf = symm_mem_world.get_buffer(peer_rank, (64,), torch.float32)
   335→        self.assertTrue(buf.eq(peer_rank).all())
   336→
   337→        # Observe a peer buffer via the subgroup
   338→        peer_rank = (subgroup.rank() + 1) % subgroup.size()
   339→        buf = symm_mem_subgroup.get_buffer(peer_rank, (64,), torch.float32)
   340→        if world.rank() < world.size() // 2:
   341→            self.assertTrue(buf.eq(peer_rank).all())
   342→        else:
   343→            self.assertTrue(buf.eq(peer_rank + world.size() // 2).all())

   345→
   346→# We move AsyncTP tests to a separate test suite because 1) Async TP ops are not
   347→# the core symmetric memory APIs, they are more like applications, 2)
   348→# MultiProcContinuousTest will skip all the following tests if a test fails (
   349→# we should fix this too). We still want to get the test signals for the core
   350→# symmetric memory APIs when Async TP ops fail.
   351→@skip_if_rocm_multiprocess  # AsyncTP is not yet supported on ROCm
   352→@instantiate_parametrized_tests
   353→@requires_cuda_p2p_access()
   354→class AsyncTPTest(MultiProcContinuousTest):
   355→    @property
   356→    def device(self) -> torch.device:
   357→        return torch.device(device_type, self.rank)
   358→
   359→    def _init_process(self):
   360→        torch.cuda.set_device(self.device)
   361→        torch.manual_seed(42 + self.rank)
   362→        torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True)
   363→        torch.set_deterministic_debug_mode("warn")
   364→        torch.utils.deterministic.fill_uninitialized_memory = True
   365→
   366→    @skipIf(
   367→        not PLATFORM_SUPPORTS_SYMM_MEM, "SymmMem is not supported on this ROCm arch"
   368→    )
   369→    @skip_if_lt_x_gpu(2)
   370→    @parametrize("gather_dim", [0, 1, 2])
   371→    def test_fused_all_gather_matmul(self, gather_dim: int) -> None:
   372→        self._init_process()
   373→
   374→        BATCH = 8
   375→        M = 64
   376→        N = 16
   377→        K = 32
   378→        group = dist.group.WORLD
   379→        rank = self.rank
   380→
   381→        torch.manual_seed(42 + rank)
   382→        A_shard_shape = [BATCH, M, K]
   383→        A_shard_shape[gather_dim] //= self.world_size
   384→
   385→        A_shard = torch.rand(A_shard_shape, device="cuda")
   386→        Bs = [torch.rand(K, N, device="cuda") for _ in range(3)]
   387→
   388→        ag_output_0, mm_outputs_0 = _fused_all_gather_matmul_fallback(
   389→            A_shard, Bs, gather_dim=gather_dim, group_name=group.group_name
   390→        )
   391→        ag_output_1, mm_outputs_1 = torch.ops.symm_mem.fused_all_gather_matmul(
   392→            A_shard, Bs, gather_dim=gather_dim, group_name=group.group_name
   393→        )
   394→
   395→        assert torch.allclose(ag_output_0, ag_output_1)
   396→        assert ag_output_0.stride() == ag_output_1.stride()
   397→        for mm_output_0, mm_output_1 in zip(mm_outputs_0, mm_outputs_1):
   398→            assert torch.allclose(mm_output_0, mm_output_1)
   399→            assert mm_output_0.stride(), mm_output_1.stride()
   400→
   401→    @skip_if_rocm_multiprocess  # this requires async_input_mm support
   402→    @skipIf(
   403→        not SM90OrLater,
   404→        "_fused_all_gather_matmul_native currently only supports sm>=90",
   405→    )
   406→    @skip_if_lt_x_gpu(2)
   407→    @parametrize("symm_mem_input", [True, False])
   408→    @parametrize("is_b_row_major", [True, False])
   409→    @skipIf(
   410→        SM100OrLater,
   411→        "https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/162917",
   412→    )
   413→    def test_fused_all_gather_matmul_native(
   414→        self, symm_mem_input: bool, is_b_row_major: bool
   415→    ) -> None:
   416→        os.environ["TORCH_SYMM_MEM_ENABLE_NATIVE_ASYNC_TP"] = "1"
   417→        self._init_process()
   418→
   419→        # See _should_use_fused_all_gather_matmul_native() for the algo
   420→        # selection criteria of _fused_all_gather_matmul_native().
   421→        M = 4096
   422→        N = 1024
   423→        K = 1024
   424→        group_name = dist.group.WORLD.group_name
   425→
   426→        torch.manual_seed(42 + self.rank)
   427→        if symm_mem_input:
   428→            A_shard = symm_mem.empty(
   429→                M // self.world_size,
   430→                K,
   431→                dtype=torch.bfloat16,
   432→                device=self.device,
   433→            ).normal_()
   434→        else:
   435→            A_shard = torch.rand(
   436→                M // self.world_size, K, dtype=torch.bfloat16, device="cuda"
   437→            )
   438→
   439→        if is_b_row_major:
   440→            B = torch.rand(K, N, dtype=torch.bfloat16, device="cuda")
   441→        else:
   442→            B = torch.rand(N, K, dtype=torch.bfloat16, device="cuda").t()
   443→
   444→        ag_baseline, mm_baseline = _fused_all_gather_matmul_fallback(
   445→            A_shard, [B], gather_dim=0, group_name=group_name
   446→        )
   447→        with torch.profiler.profile(
   448→            activities=[
   449→                torch.profiler.ProfilerActivity.CUDA,
   450→            ],
   451→        ) as prof:
   452→            ag_target, mm_target = torch.ops.symm_mem.fused_all_gather_matmul(
   453→                A_shard, [B], gather_dim=0, group_name=group_name
   454→            )
   455→
   456→        self.assertTrue(
   457→            any("PersistentAsyncInputScheduler" in event.key for event in prof.events())
   458→        )
   459→
   460→        torch.testing.assert_close(ag_target, ag_baseline)
   461→        torch.testing.assert_close(mm_target[0], mm_baseline[0])
   462→        os.environ["TORCH_SYMM_MEM_ENABLE_NATIVE_ASYNC_TP"] = "0"

   502→    @skipIf(
   503→        not PLATFORM_SUPPORTS_SYMM_MEM, "SymmMem is not supported on this ROCm arch"
   504→    )
   505→    @skip_if_lt_x_gpu(2)
   506→    @skipUnless(SM89OrLater, "Requires compute capability >= 8.9")
   507→    @parametrize("gather_dim", [0, 1])
   508→    @parametrize(
   509→        "scale_mode", ["tensor-wise", "row-wise-replicated", "row-wise-sharded"]
   510→    )
   511→    def test_fused_all_gather_scaled_matmul(
   512→        self, gather_dim: int, scale_mode: str
   513→    ) -> None:
   514→        self._init_process()
   515→
   516→        BATCH = 8
   517→        M = 64
   518→        N = 16
   519→        K = 32
   520→        group = dist.group.WORLD
   521→        rank = self.rank
   522→
   523→        if gather_dim == 0:
   524→            leading_dims = (BATCH // self.world_size, M)
   525→        elif gather_dim == 1:
   526→            leading_dims = (BATCH, M // self.world_size)
   527→        else:
   528→            raise AssertionError(f"Invalid scale_mode: {scale_mode}")
   529→
   530→        torch.manual_seed(42 + rank)
   531→
   532→        A_shard = torch.rand(*leading_dims, K, device="cuda").to(e4m3_type)
   533→        Bs = [torch.rand(N, K, device="cuda").to(e4m3_type).T for _ in range(3)]
   534→
   535→        if scale_mode == "tensor-wise":
   536→            A_scale = torch.tensor(0.1, device="cuda")
   537→            B_scales = [torch.tensor(0.1, device="cuda") for _ in range(3)]
   538→            out_dtypes = [None, torch.bfloat16, torch.float32]
   539→        elif scale_mode == "row-wise-sharded":
   540→            A_scale = torch.full((*leading_dims, 1), 0.1, device="cuda")
   541→            B_scales = [torch.full((1, N), 0.1, device="cuda") for _ in range(3)]
   542→            out_dtypes = [torch.bfloat16] * 3
   543→        elif scale_mode == "row-wise-replicated":
   544→            A_scale = torch.full((BATCH, M, 1), 0.1, device="cuda")
   545→            B_scales = [torch.full((1, N), 0.1, device="cuda") for _ in range(3)]
   546→            out_dtypes = [torch.bfloat16] * 3
   547→        else:
   548→            raise AssertionError(f"Invalid scale_mode: {scale_mode}")
   549→
   550→        ag_output_0, mm_outputs_0 = _fused_all_gather_scaled_matmul_fallback(
   551→            A_shard,
   552→            Bs,
   553→            A_scale,
   554→            B_scales,
   555→            gather_dim=gather_dim,
   556→            group_name=group.group_name,
   557→            biases=[None] * len(Bs),
   558→            result_scales=[None] * len(Bs),
   559→            out_dtypes=out_dtypes,
   560→            use_fast_accum=[None] * len(Bs),
   561→        )
   562→        ag_output_1, mm_outputs_1 = torch.ops.symm_mem.fused_all_gather_scaled_matmul(
   563→            A_shard,
   564→            Bs,
   565→            A_scale,
   566→            B_scales,
   567→            gather_dim=gather_dim,
   568→            group_name=group.group_name,
   569→            biases=[None] * len(Bs),
   570→            result_scales=[None] * len(Bs),
   571→            out_dtypes=out_dtypes,
   572→            use_fast_accum=[None] * len(Bs),
   573→        )
   574→
   575→        self.assertTrue(
   576→            torch.allclose(
   577→                ag_output_0.to(torch.float32),
   578→                ag_output_1.to(torch.float32),
   579→            )
   580→        )
   581→        self.assertEqual(ag_output_0.stride(), ag_output_1.stride())
   582→        for mm_output_0, mm_output_1 in zip(mm_outputs_0, mm_outputs_1):
   583→            self.assertTrue(
   584→                torch.allclose(
   585→                    mm_output_0.to(torch.float32), mm_output_1.to(torch.float32)
   586→                )
   587→            )
   588→            self.assertEqual(mm_output_0.stride(), mm_output_1.stride())
   589→            self.assertEqual(mm_output_0.dtype, mm_output_1.dtype)
   590→

   623→    @parametrize("scatter_dim", [0, 1])
   624→    @parametrize("rowwise", [True, False])
   625→    @skipIf(
   626→        SM100OrLater,
   627→        "https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/162940",
   628→    )
   629→    def test_fused_scaled_matmul_reduce_scatter(
   630→        self, scatter_dim: int, rowwise: bool
   631→    ) -> None:
   632→        self._init_process()
   633→
   634→        BATCH = 8
   635→        M = 64
   636→        N = 16
   637→        K = 32
   638→        group = dist.group.WORLD
   639→        rank = self.rank
   640→
   641→        torch.manual_seed(42 + rank)
   642→        A = torch.rand(BATCH, M, K, device="cuda").to(e4m3_type)
   643→        B = torch.rand(N, K, device="cuda").to(e4m3_type).T
   644→
   645→        if rowwise:
   646→            A_scale = torch.full((BATCH, M, 1), 0.1, device="cuda")
   647→            B_scale = torch.full((1, N), 0.1, device="cuda")
   648→        else:
   649→            A_scale = torch.tensor(0.1, device="cuda")
   650→            B_scale = torch.tensor(0.1, device="cuda")
   651→
   652→        output_shape = [*A.shape[:-1], B.shape[1]]
   653→
   654→        outputs = []
   655→        for context in test_contexts:
   656→            with context():
   657→                outputs.append(
   658→                    torch.ops.symm_mem.fused_scaled_matmul_reduce_scatter(
   659→                        A,
   660→                        B,
   661→                        A_scale,
   662→                        B_scale,
   663→                        "avg",
   664→                        scatter_dim,
   665→                        scatter_dim,
   666→                        group.group_name,
   667→                        output_shape,
   668→                        out_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
   669→                    )
   670→                )
   671→
   672→        assert outputs[0].stride() == outputs[1].stride()
   673→        self.assertEqual(outputs[0], outputs[1])

   682→        x = restride_A_shard_for_fused_all_gather_matmul(t, dim)
   683→        self.assertTrue(x.movedim(dim, 0).is_contiguous())

   691→# [READ ME FIRST]
   692→# The `SymmMemEmptySetDeviceTest` suite parameterizes whether user sets the
   693→# device before calling symm_mem.empty.  Either way should work.
   694→# However, since `set_device` is persistent, we cannot use the
   695→# `MultiProcContinuousTest` template because the next function will be
   696→# "contaminated", leading to flaky tests (e.g. hang). Therefore, we use
   697→# `MultiProcessTestCase` which spawns new processes for each test function.
   698→# Please limit the number of tests you want to add under this test
   699→# suite as respawning processes and `init_process_group` is expensive.
   700→@instantiate_parametrized_tests
   701→@requires_cuda_p2p_access()
   702→class SymmMemEmptySetDeviceTest(MultiProcessTestCase):
   703→    def setUp(self) -> None:
   704→        super().setUp()
   705→        self._spawn_processes()
   706→
   707→    @property
   708→    def world_size(self) -> int:
   709→        return device_module.device_count()
   710→
   711→    @property
   712→    def device(self) -> torch.device:
   713→        return torch.device(device_type, self.rank)
   714→
   715→    def _init_process(self, set_device: bool):
   716→        if set_device:
   717→            torch.cuda.set_device(self.device)
   718→        store = dist.FileStore(self.file_name, self.world_size)
   719→        dist.init_process_group(
   720→            backend="nccl",
   721→            world_size=self.world_size,
   722→            rank=self.rank,
   723→            store=store,
   724→        )
   725→        torch.manual_seed(42 + self.rank)
   726→
   727→    def _get_test_alloc_args(self):
   728→        shape = (64, 64)
   729→        stride = (64, 1)
   730→        dtype = torch.float32
   731→        device = self.device
   732→        return (shape, stride, dtype, device)
   733→
   734→    def _verify_symmetric_memory(self, symm_mem_hdl):
   735→        self.assertEqual(symm_mem_hdl.world_size, self.world_size)
   736→
   737→        buf = symm_mem_hdl.get_buffer(
   738→            0, (symm_mem_hdl.buffer_size // 4,), torch.float32
   739→        )
   740→        self.assertEqual(buf.storage_offset(), 0)
   741→        self.assertEqual(buf.untyped_storage().size(), symm_mem_hdl.buffer_size)
   742→
   743→        if symm_mem_hdl.rank == 0:
   744→            symm_mem_hdl.wait_signal(src_rank=1)
   745→            self.assertTrue(buf.eq(42).all())
   746→        else:
   747→            buf.fill_(42)
   748→            symm_mem_hdl.put_signal(dst_rank=0)
   749→
   750→        symm_mem_hdl.barrier()
   751→
   752→        if symm_mem_hdl.rank == 0:
   753→            symm_mem_hdl.barrier()
   754→            self.assertTrue(buf.eq(43).all())
   755→        else:
   756→            buf.fill_(43)
   757→            symm_mem_hdl.barrier()
   758→
   759→        symm_mem_hdl.barrier()
   760→
   761→    @skipIf(
   762→        not PLATFORM_SUPPORTS_SYMM_MEM, "SymmMem is not supported on this ROCm arch"
   763→    )
   764→    @skip_if_lt_x_gpu(2)
   765→    @parametrize("set_device", [True, False])
   766→    def test_empty_strided_p2p(self, set_device: bool) -> None:
   767→        self._init_process(set_device)
   768→        group_name = dist.group.WORLD.group_name
   769→        enable_symm_mem_for_group(group_name)
   770→
   771→        alloc_args = self._get_test_alloc_args()
   772→
   773→        t = torch.empty((64, 64), device=self.device)
   774→        self.assertIsNone(_SymmetricMemory.rendezvous(t))
   775→
   776→        t = _SymmetricMemory.empty_strided_p2p(*alloc_args, group_name=group_name)
   777→        symm_mem_hdl = _SymmetricMemory.rendezvous(t)
   778→
   779→        del t
   780→        self._verify_symmetric_memory(symm_mem_hdl)
   781→
   782→    @skipIf(
   783→        not PLATFORM_SUPPORTS_SYMM_MEM, "SymmMem is not supported on this ROCm arch"
   784→    )
   785→    @skip_if_rocm_ver_lessthan_multiprocess((7, 0))
   786→    @skip_if_lt_x_gpu(2)
   787→    @parametrize("set_device", [True, False])
   788→    def test_empty_strided_p2p_persistent(self, set_device: bool) -> None:
   789→        self._init_process(set_device)
   790→        group_name = dist.group.WORLD.group_name
   791→        enable_symm_mem_for_group(group_name)
   792→
   793→        alloc_args = self._get_test_alloc_args()
   794→
   795→        alloc_id = 42 + random.randint(0, 2147483647)
   796→        t = _SymmetricMemory.empty_strided_p2p(
   797→            *alloc_args, group_name=group_name, alloc_id=alloc_id
   798→        )
   799→        data_ptr = t.data_ptr()
   800→
   801→        # Verify that persistent allocation would fail if there's an active
   802→        # allocation with the same alloc_id.
   803→        with self.assertRaises(RuntimeError):
   804→            _SymmetricMemory.empty_strided_p2p(
   805→                *alloc_args, group_name=group_name, alloc_id=alloc_id
   806→            )
   807→
   808→        # Verify that persistent allocation would succeed in lieu of activate
   809→        # allocations with the same alloc_id, and the returned tensor would
   810→        # have the same data pointer.
   811→        del t
   812→        t = _SymmetricMemory.empty_strided_p2p(
   813→            *alloc_args, group_name=group_name, alloc_id=alloc_id
   814→        )
   815→        self.assertEqual(t.data_ptr(), data_ptr)
   816→
   817→        symm_mem_hdl = _SymmetricMemory.rendezvous(t)
   818→        self._verify_symmetric_memory(symm_mem_hdl)

   821→# This Test class is used to test the error handling of SymmetricMemory APIs.
   822→# Since a process restart is often needed after each test, we use the
   823→# MultiProcessTestCase instead of MultiProcContinuousTest.
   824→@requires_cuda_p2p_access()
   825→class SymmMemNegativeTest(MultiProcessTestCase):
   826→    def setUp(self) -> None:
   827→        super().setUp()
   828→        self._spawn_processes()
   829→
   830→    @property
   831→    def world_size(self) -> int:
   832→        return device_module.device_count()
   833→
   834→    @property
   835→    def device(self) -> torch.device:
   836→        return torch.device(device_type, self.rank)
   837→
   838→    def _init_process(self):
   839→        torch.cuda.set_device(self.device)
   840→        store = dist.FileStore(self.file_name, self.world_size)
   841→        dist.init_process_group(
   842→            backend="nccl",
   843→            world_size=self.world_size,
   844→            rank=self.rank,
   845→            store=store,
   846→        )
   847→        torch.manual_seed(42 + self.rank)
   848→
   849→    # These timeout tests are skipped on ROCm because timeout calls trap(), which
   850→    # is handled differently inside hip runtime. It collects gpu coredump and causes
   851→    # the linux kernel to create a core dump of the host application. The functionality
   852→    # is there, meaning timeout is happening correctly. However, there isn't a nice way
   853→    # to test it as the current executing thread will coredump and exit.
   854→    @skip_if_rocm_multiprocess
   855→    @skip_if_lt_x_gpu(2)
   856→    def test_barrier_timeout(self) -> None:
   857→        self._init_process()
   858→
   859→        t = symm_mem.empty(1, device="cuda")
   860→        symm_mem_hdl = symm_mem.rendezvous(t, group=dist.group.WORLD)
   861→
   862→        if self.rank == 0:
   863→            with self.assertRaises(RuntimeError):
   864→                symm_mem_hdl.barrier(timeout_ms=1000)
   865→                torch.cuda.synchronize()
   866→        else:
   867→            torch.cuda.synchronize()
   868→
   869→        # The device-side timeout triggers a __trap() that causes all
   870→        # subsequent host/device interactions to result in an "unspecified
   871→        # launch failure." Using os._exit(0) to abort the test, as it's
   872→        # impossible to terminate the process in this state.
   873→        os._exit(0)
   874→
   875→    # These timeout tests are skipped on ROCm because timeout calls trap(), which
   876→    # is handled differently inside hip runtime. It collects gpu coredump and causes
   877→    # the linux kernel to create a core dump of the host application. The functionality
   878→    # is there, meaning timeout is happening correctly. However, there isn't a nice way
   879→    # to test it as the current executing thread will coredump and exit.
   880→    @skip_if_rocm_multiprocess
   881→    @skip_if_lt_x_gpu(2)
   882→    def test_put_signal_timeout(self) -> None:
   883→        self._init_process()
   884→
   885→        t = symm_mem.empty(1, device="cuda")
   886→        symm_mem_hdl = symm_mem.rendezvous(t, group=dist.group.WORLD)
   887→
   888→        if self.rank == 0:
   889→            with self.assertRaises(RuntimeError):
   890→                # First, put a signal into rank 1's signal pad. Since rank 1
   891→                # doesn't wait on this signal, the subsequent put will timeout.
   892→                symm_mem_hdl.put_signal(dst_rank=1)
   893→                symm_mem_hdl.put_signal(dst_rank=1, timeout_ms=1000)
   894→                torch.cuda.synchronize()
   895→        else:
   896→            torch.cuda.synchronize()
   897→
   898→        # The device-side timeout triggers a __trap() that causes all
   899→        # subsequent host/device interactions to result in an "unspecified
   900→        # launch failure." Using os._exit(0) to abort the test, as it's
   901→        # impossible to terminate the process in this state.
   902→        os._exit(0)

   933→class SymmMemCollectiveTest(MultiProcContinuousTest):
   934→    @property

   937→

   943→    @requires_multicast_support()
   944→    @parametrize("dtype", [torch.float, torch.bfloat16])
   945→    @parametrize("align_bytes", [4, 8, 16])
   946→    @parametrize("size_bytes", [4, 8192, 8196])
   947→    def test_multimem_all_reduce(
   948→        self, dtype: torch.dtype, size_bytes: int, align_bytes: int
   949→    ) -> None:
   950→        self._init_process()
   951→        group_name = dist.group.WORLD.group_name
   952→
   953→        t = symm_mem.empty((16384), dtype=dtype, device=self.device)
   954→        symm_mem.rendezvous(t, group=dist.group.WORLD)
   955→
   956→        self.assertTrue(t.data_ptr() % 16 == 0)
   957→        self.assertTrue(align_bytes % t.element_size() == 0)
   958→        self.assertTrue(size_bytes % t.element_size() == 0)
   959→
   960→        shift = align_bytes // t.element_size()
   961→        numel = size_bytes // t.element_size()
   962→        res = t[shift : shift + numel]
   963→        res.normal_()
   964→        inp = res.clone()
   965→
   966→        torch.ops.symm_mem.multimem_all_reduce_(res, "sum", group_name)
   967→
   968→        # Head and tail should not be written
   969→        self.assertTrue(t[:shift].eq(0).all().item())
   970→        self.assertTrue(t[shift + numel :].eq(0).all().item())
   971→        self._verify_all_reduce_result(inp, res)

  1039→        for dtype, size_bytes, align_bytes, copy, offset in itertools.product(

  1059→                res = torch.ops.symm_mem.one_shot_all_reduce_copy(

  1068→    def test_two_shot_all_reduce(self) -> None:
  1069→        self._init_process()
  1070→        group_name = dist.group.WORLD.group_name
  1071→
  1072→        for dtype, size_bytes, align_bytes, inplace in itertools.product(
  1073→            [torch.float, torch.bfloat16],
  1074→            [4, 8192, 8196],
  1075→            [
  1076→                8
  1077→            ],  # TODO: add back [4, 8, 16], currently OOM when looping over all combinations
  1078→            [True, False],
  1079→        ):
  1080→            t = symm_mem.empty(16384, dtype=dtype, device=self.device).fill_(0)
  1081→            symm_mem.rendezvous(t, group=group_name)
  1082→
  1083→            self.assertTrue(t.data_ptr() % 16 == 0)
  1084→            self.assertTrue(align_bytes % t.element_size() == 0)
  1085→            self.assertTrue(size_bytes % t.element_size() == 0)
  1086→
  1087→            shift = align_bytes // t.element_size()
  1088→            numel = size_bytes // t.element_size()
  1089→            res = t[shift : shift + numel]
  1090→            res.normal_()
  1091→            inp = res.clone()
  1092→            if not inplace:
  1093→                out = torch.empty_like(inp)
  1094→                torch.ops.symm_mem.two_shot_all_reduce_out(res, "sum", group_name, out)
  1095→            else:
  1096→                torch.ops.symm_mem.two_shot_all_reduce_(res, "sum", group_name)
  1097→
  1098→            # Head and tail should not be written
  1099→            self.assertTrue(t[:shift].eq(0).all().item())
  1100→            self.assertTrue(t[shift + numel :].eq(0).all().item())
  1101→            self._verify_all_reduce_result(inp, res if inplace else out)

  1103→    def _verify_all_reduce_result(self, inp, res):
  1104→        gathered_res = all_gather_tensor(res, 0, "0").view(self.world_size, -1)
  1105→        # Verify that the results across ranks are identical
  1106→        self.assertEqual(
  1107→            (gathered_res == gathered_res[0, :]).all(dim=0).sum(), inp.numel()
  1108→        )
  1109→
  1110→        # Verify that the result are close to the sum of inputs across ranks

1→```{eval-rst}
     2→.. role:: hidden
     3→    :class: hidden-section
     4→```
     5→
     6→# Distributed communication package - torch.distributed
     7→
     8→:::{note}
     9→Please refer to [PyTorch Distributed Overview](https://pytorch.org/tutorials/beginner/dist_overview.html)
    10→for a brief introduction to all features related to distributed training.
    11→:::
    12→
    13→```{eval-rst}
    14→.. automodule:: torch.distributed
    15→```
    16→
    17→```{eval-rst}
    18→.. currentmodule:: torch.distributed
    19→```
    20→
    21→## Backends
    22→
    23→`torch.distributed` supports four built-in backends, each with
    24→different capabilities. The table below shows which functions are available
    25→for use with a CPU or GPU for each backend. For NCCL, GPU refers to CUDA GPU
    26→while for XCCL to XPU GPU.
    27→
    28→MPI supports CUDA only if the implementation used to build PyTorch supports it.
    29→
    30→```{eval-rst}
    31→+----------------+-----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+
    32→| Backend        | ``gloo``  | ``mpi``   | ``nccl``  | ``xccl``  |
    33→+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+
    34→| Device         | CPU | GPU | CPU | GPU | CPU | GPU | CPU | GPU |
    35→+================+=====+=====+=====+=====+=====+=====+=====+=====+
    36→| send           | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   | ?   | ✘   | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   |
    37→+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+
    38→| recv           | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   | ?   | ✘   | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   |
    39→+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+
    40→| broadcast      | ✓   | ✓   | ✓   | ?   | ✘   | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   |
    41→+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+
    42→| all_reduce     | ✓   | ✓   | ✓   | ?   | ✘   | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   |
    43→+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+
    44→| reduce         | ✓   | ✓   | ✓   | ?   | ✘   | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   |
    45→+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+
    46→| all_gather     | ✓   | ✓   | ✓   | ?   | ✘   | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   |
    47→+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+
    48→| gather         | ✓   | ✓   | ✓   | ?   | ✘   | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   |
    49→+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+
    50→| scatter        | ✓   | ✓   | ✓   | ?   | ✘   | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   |
    51→+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+
    52→| reduce_scatter | ✓   | ✓   | ✘   | ✘   | ✘   | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   |
    53→+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+
    54→| all_to_all     | ✘   | ✘   | ✓   | ?   | ✘   | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   |
    55→+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+
    56→| barrier        | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   | ?   | ✘   | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   |
    57→+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+
    58→```
    59→
    60→### Backends that come with PyTorch
    61→
    62→PyTorch distributed package supports Linux (stable), macOS (stable), and Windows (prototype).
    63→By default for Linux, the Gloo and NCCL backends are built and included in PyTorch
    64→distributed (NCCL only when building with CUDA). MPI is an optional backend that can only be
    65→included if you build PyTorch from source. (e.g. building PyTorch on a host that has MPI
    66→installed.)
    67→
    68→:::{note}
    69→As of PyTorch v1.8, Windows supports all collective communications backends but NCCL,
    70→If the `init_method` argument of {func}`init_process_group` points to a file it must adhere
    71→to the following schema:
    72→
    73→- Local file system, `init_method="file:///d:/tmp/some_file"`
    74→- Shared file system, `init_method="file://////{machine_name}/{share_folder_name}/some_file"`
    75→
    76→Same as on Linux platform, you can enable TcpStore by setting environment variables,
    77→MASTER_ADDR and MASTER_PORT.
    78→:::
    79→
    80→### Which backend to use?
    81→
    82→In the past, we were often asked: "which backend should I use?".
    83→
    84→- Rule of thumb
    85→
    86→  - Use the NCCL backend for distributed training with CUDA **GPU**.
    87→  - Use the XCCL backend for distributed training with XPU **GPU**.
    88→  - Use the Gloo backend for distributed training with **CPU**.
    89→
    90→- GPU hosts with InfiniBand interconnect
    91→
    92→  - Use NCCL, since it's the only backend that currently supports
    93→    InfiniBand and GPUDirect.
    94→
    95→- GPU hosts with Ethernet interconnect
    96→
    97→  - Use NCCL, since it currently provides the best distributed GPU
    98→    training performance, especially for multiprocess single-node or
    99→    multi-node distributed training. If you encounter any problem with
   100→    NCCL, use Gloo as the fallback option. (Note that Gloo currently
   101→    runs slower than NCCL for GPUs.)
   102→
   103→- CPU hosts with InfiniBand interconnect
   104→
   105→  - If your InfiniBand has enabled IP over IB, use Gloo, otherwise,
   106→    use MPI instead. We are planning on adding InfiniBand support for
   107→    Gloo in the upcoming releases.
   108→
   109→- CPU hosts with Ethernet interconnect
   110→
   111→  - Use Gloo, unless you have specific reasons to use MPI.
   112→
   113→### Common environment variables
   114→
   115→#### Choosing the network interface to use
   116→
   117→By default, both the NCCL and Gloo backends will try to find the right network interface to use.
   118→If the automatically detected interface is not correct, you can override it using the following
   119→environment variables (applicable to the respective backend):
   120→
   121→- **NCCL_SOCKET_IFNAME**, for example `export NCCL_SOCKET_IFNAME=eth0`
   122→- **GLOO_SOCKET_IFNAME**, for example `export GLOO_SOCKET_IFNAME=eth0`
   123→
   124→If you're using the Gloo backend, you can specify multiple interfaces by separating
   125→them by a comma, like this: `export GLOO_SOCKET_IFNAME=eth0,eth1,eth2,eth3`.
   126→The backend will dispatch operations in a round-robin fashion across these interfaces.
   127→It is imperative that all processes specify the same number of interfaces in this variable.
   128→
   129→#### Other NCCL environment variables
   130→
   131→**Debugging** - in case of NCCL failure, you can set `NCCL_DEBUG=INFO` to print an explicit
   132→warning message as well as basic NCCL initialization information.
   133→
   134→You may also use `NCCL_DEBUG_SUBSYS` to get more details about a specific
   135→aspect of NCCL. For example, `NCCL_DEBUG_SUBSYS=COLL` would print logs of
   136→collective calls, which may be helpful when debugging hangs, especially those
   137→caused by collective type or message size mismatch. In case of topology
   138→detection failure, it would be helpful to set `NCCL_DEBUG_SUBSYS=GRAPH`
   139→to inspect the detailed detection result and save as reference if further help
   140→from NCCL team is needed.
   141→
   142→**Performance tuning** - NCCL performs automatic tuning based on its topology detection to save users'
   143→tuning effort. On some socket-based systems, users may still try tuning
   144→`NCCL_SOCKET_NTHREADS` and `NCCL_NSOCKS_PERTHREAD` to increase socket
   145→network bandwidth. These two environment variables have been pre-tuned by NCCL
   146→for some cloud providers, such as AWS or GCP.
   147→
   148→For a full list of NCCL environment variables, please refer to
   149→[NVIDIA NCCL's official documentation](https://docs.nvidia.com/deeplearning/nccl/user-guide/docs/env.html)
   150→
   151→You can tune NCCL communicators even further using `torch.distributed.ProcessGroupNCCL.NCCLConfig`
   152→and `torch.distributed.ProcessGroupNCCL.Options`. Learn more about them using `help`
   153→(e.g. `help(torch.distributed.ProcessGroupNCCL.NCCLConfig)`) in the interpreter.
   154→
   155→(distributed-basics)=
   156→
   157→## Basics
   158→
   159→The `torch.distributed` package provides PyTorch support and communication primitives
   160→for multiprocess parallelism across several computation nodes running on one or more
   161→machines. The class {func}`torch.nn.parallel.DistributedDataParallel` builds on this
   162→functionality to provide synchronous distributed training as a wrapper around any
   163→PyTorch model. This differs from the kinds of parallelism provided by
   164→{doc}`multiprocessing` and {func}`torch.nn.DataParallel` in that it supports
   165→multiple network-connected machines and in that the user must explicitly launch a separate
   166→copy of the main training script for each process.
   167→
   168→In the single-machine synchronous case, `torch.distributed` or the
   169→{func}`torch.nn.parallel.DistributedDataParallel` wrapper may still have advantages over other
   170→approaches to data-parallelism, including {func}`torch.nn.DataParallel`:
   171→
   172→- Each process maintains its own optimizer and performs a complete optimization step with each
   173→  iteration. While this may appear redundant, since the gradients have already been gathered
   174→  together and averaged across processes and are thus the same for every process, this means
   175→  that no parameter broadcast step is needed, reducing time spent transferring tensors between
   176→  nodes.
   177→- Each process contains an independent Python interpreter, eliminating the extra interpreter
   178→  overhead and "GIL-thrashing" that comes from driving several execution threads, model
   179→  replicas, or GPUs from a single Python process. This is especially important for models that
   180→  make heavy use of the Python runtime, including models with recurrent layers or many small
   181→  components.
   182→
   183→## Initialization
   184→
   185→The package needs to be initialized using the {func}`torch.distributed.init_process_group`
   186→or {func}`torch.distributed.device_mesh.init_device_mesh` function before calling any other methods.
   187→Both block until all processes have joined.
   188→
   189→:::{warning}
   190→Initialization is not thread-safe. Process group creation should be performed from a single thread, to prevent
   191→inconsistent 'UUID' assignment across ranks, and to prevent races during initialization that can lead to hangs.

[metadata] {"type": "text", "file": {"filePath": "/workspace/docs/source/distributed.md", "content": "```{eval-rst}\n.. role:: hidden\n    :class: hidden-section\n```\n\n# Distributed communication package - torch.distributed\n\n:::{note}\nPlease refer to [PyTorch Distributed Overview](https://pytorch.org/tutorials/beginner/dist_overview.html)\nfor a brief introduction to all features related to distributed training.\n:::\n\n```{eval-rst}\n.. automodule:: torch.distributed\n```\n\n```{eval-rst}\n.. currentmodule:: torch.distributed\n```\n\n## Backends\n\n`torch.distributed` supports four built-in backends, each with\ndifferent capabilities. The table below shows which functions are available\nfor use with a CPU or GPU for each backend. For NCCL, GPU refers to CUDA GPU\nwhile for XCCL to XPU GPU.\n\nMPI supports CUDA only if the implementation used to build PyTorch supports it.\n\n```{eval-rst}\n+----------------+-----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+\n| Backend        | ``gloo``  | ``mpi``   | ``nccl``  | ``xccl``  |\n+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+\n| Device         | CPU | GPU | CPU | GPU | CPU | GPU | CPU | GPU |\n+================+=====+=====+=====+=====+=====+=====+=====+=====+\n| send           | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   | ?   | ✘   | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   |\n+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+\n| recv           | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   | ?   | ✘   | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   |\n+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+\n| broadcast      | ✓   | ✓   | ✓   | ?   | ✘   | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   |\n+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+\n| all_reduce     | ✓   | ✓   | ✓   | ?   | ✘   | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   |\n+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+\n| reduce         | ✓   | ✓   | ✓   | ?   | ✘   | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   |\n+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+\n| all_gather     | ✓   | ✓   | ✓   | ?   | ✘   | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   |\n+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+\n| gather         | ✓   | ✓   | ✓   | ?   | ✘   | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   |\n+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+\n| scatter        | ✓   | ✓   | ✓   | ?   | ✘   | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   |\n+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+\n| reduce_scatter | ✓   | ✓   | ✘   | ✘   | ✘   | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   |\n+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+\n| all_to_all     | ✘   | ✘   | ✓   | ?   | ✘   | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   |\n+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+\n| barrier        | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   | ?   | ✘   | ✓   | ✘   | ✓   |\n+----------------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+\n```\n\n### Backends that come with PyTorch\n\nPyTorch distributed package supports Linux (stable), macOS (stable), and Windows (prototype).\nBy default for Linux, the Gloo and NCCL backends are built and included in PyTorch\ndistributed (NCCL only when building with CUDA). MPI is an optional backend that can only be\nincluded if you build PyTorch from source. (e.g. building PyTorch on a host that has MPI\ninstalled.)\n\n:::{note}\nAs of PyTorch v1.8, Windows supports all collective communications backends but NCCL,\nIf the `init_method` argument of {func}`init_process_group` points to a file it must adhere\nto the following schema:\n\n- Local file system, `init_method=\"file:///d:/tmp/some_file\"`\n- Shared file system, `init_method=\"file://////{machine_name}/{share_folder_name}/some_file\"`\n\nSame as on Linux platform, you can enable TcpStore by setting environment variables,\nMASTER_ADDR and MASTER_PORT.\n:::\n\n### Which backend to use?\n\nIn the past, we were often asked: \"which backend should I use?\".\n\n- Rule of thumb\n\n  - Use the NCCL backend for distributed training with CUDA **GPU**.\n  - Use the XCCL backend for distributed training with XPU **GPU**.\n  - Use the Gloo backend for distributed training with **CPU**.\n\n- GPU hosts with InfiniBand interconnect\n\n  - Use NCCL, since it's the only backend that currently supports\n    InfiniBand and GPUDirect.\n\n- GPU hosts with Ethernet interconnect\n\n  - Use NCCL, since it currently provides the best distributed GPU\n    training performance, especially for multiprocess single-node or\n    multi-node distributed training. If you encounter any problem with\n    NCCL, use Gloo as the fallback option. (Note that Gloo currently\n    runs slower than NCCL for GPUs.)\n\n- CPU hosts with InfiniBand interconnect\n\n  - If your InfiniBand has enabled IP over IB, use Gloo, otherwise,\n    use MPI instead. We are planning on adding InfiniBand support for\n    Gloo in the upcoming releases.\n\n- CPU hosts with Ethernet interconnect\n\n  - Use Gloo, unless you have specific reasons to use MPI.\n\n### Common environment variables\n\n#### Choosing the network interface to use\n\nBy default, both the NCCL and Gloo backends will try to find the right network interface to use.\nIf the automatically detected interface is not correct, you can override it using the following\nenvironment variables (applicable to the respective backend):\n\n- **NCCL_SOCKET_IFNAME**, for example `export NCCL_SOCKET_IFNAME=eth0`\n- **GLOO_SOCKET_IFNAME**, for example `export GLOO_SOCKET_IFNAME=eth0`\n\nIf you're using the Gloo backend, you can specify multiple interfaces by separating\nthem by a comma, like this: `export GLOO_SOCKET_IFNAME=eth0,eth1,eth2,eth3`.\nThe backend will dispatch operations in a round-robin fashion across these interfaces.\nIt is imperative that all processes specify the same number of interfaces in this variable.\n\n#### Other NCCL environment variables\n\n**Debugging** - in case of NCCL failure, you can set `NCCL_DEBUG=INFO` to print an explicit\nwarning message as well as basic NCCL initialization information.\n\nYou may also use `NCCL_DEBUG_SUBSYS` to get more details about a specific\naspect of NCCL. For example, `NCCL_DEBUG_SUBSYS=COLL` would print logs of\ncollective calls, which may be helpful when debugging hangs, especially those\ncaused by collective type or message size mismatch. In case of topology\ndetection failure, it would be helpful to set `NCCL_DEBUG_SUBSYS=GRAPH`\nto inspect the detailed detection result and save as reference if further help\nfrom NCCL team is needed.\n\n**Performance tuning** - NCCL performs automatic tuning based on its topology detection to save users'\ntuning effort. On some socket-based systems, users may still try tuning\n`NCCL_SOCKET_NTHREADS` and `NCCL_NSOCKS_PERTHREAD` to increase socket\nnetwork bandwidth. These two environment variables have been pre-tuned by NCCL\nfor some cloud providers, such as AWS or GCP.\n\nFor a full list of NCCL environment variables, please refer to\n[NVIDIA NCCL's official documentation](https://docs.nvidia.com/deeplearning/nccl/user-guide/docs/env.html)\n\nYou can tune NCCL communicators even further using `torch.distributed.ProcessGroupNCCL.NCCLConfig`\nand `torch.distributed.ProcessGroupNCCL.Options`. Learn more about them using `help`\n(e.g. `help(torch.distributed.ProcessGroupNCCL.NCCLConfig)`) in the interpreter.\n\n(distributed-basics)=\n\n## Basics\n\nThe `torch.distributed` package provides PyTorch support and communication primitives\nfor multiprocess parallelism across several computation nodes running on one or more\nmachines. The class {func}`torch.nn.parallel.DistributedDataParallel` builds on this\nfunctionality to provide synchronous distributed training as a wrapper around any\nPyTorch model. This differs from the kinds of parallelism provided by\n{doc}`multiprocessing` and {func}`torch.nn.DataParallel` in that it supports\nmultiple network-connected machines and in that the user must explicitly launch a separate\ncopy of the main training script for each process.\n\nIn the single-machine synchronous case, `torch.distributed` or the\n{func}`torch.nn.parallel.DistributedDataParallel` wrapper may still have advantages over other\napproaches to data-parallelism, including {func}`torch.nn.DataParallel`:\n\n- Each process maintains its own optimizer and performs a complete optimization step with each\n  iteration. While this may appear redundant, since the gradients have already been gathered\n  together and averaged across processes and are thus the same for every process, this means\n  that no parameter broadcast step is needed, reducing time spent transferring tensors between\n  nodes.\n- Each process contains an independent Python interpreter, eliminating the extra interpreter\n  overhead and \"GIL-thrashing\" that comes from driving several execution threads, model\n  replicas, or GPUs from a single Python process. This is especially important for models that\n  make heavy use of the Python runtime, including models with recurrent layers or many small\n  components.\n\n## Initialization\n\nThe package needs to be initialized using the {func}`torch.distributed.init_process_group`\nor {func}`torch.distributed.device_mesh.init_device_mesh` function before calling any other methods.\nBoth block until all processes have joined.\n\n:::{warning}\nInitialization is not thread-safe. Process group creation should be performed from a single thread, to prevent\ninconsistent 'UUID' assignment across ranks, and to prevent races during initialization that can lead to hangs.\n:::\n\n```{eval-rst}\n.. autofunction:: is_available\n```\n\n```{eval-rst}\n.. autofunction:: init_process_group\n```", "numLines": 200, "startLine": 1, "totalLines": 1523}}

# This makefile does nothing but delegating the actual building to cmake.

SHELL        = /bin/bash
.SHELLFLAGS := -eu -o pipefail -c
PYTHON      ?= $(shell command -v python3 || command -v python)
PIP          = $(PYTHON) -m pip
NIGHTLY_TOOL_OPTS := pull

.PHONY: all
all:
	@cmake -S . -B build $(shell $(PYTHON) ./scripts/get_python_cmake_flags.py) && \
		cmake --build build --parallel --

.PHONY: triton
triton:
	$(PIP) uninstall -y triton
	@./scripts/install_triton_wheel.sh

.PHONY: clean
clean: # This will remove ALL build folders.
	@rm -r build*/ || true

.PHONY: linecount
linecount:
	@cloc --read-lang-def=caffe.cloc caffe2 || \
		echo "Cloc is not available on the machine. You can install cloc with " && \
		echo "    sudo apt-get install cloc"

.PHONY: ensure-branch-clean
ensure-branch-clean:
	@if [ -n "$(shell git status --porcelain)" ]; then \
		echo "Please commit or stash all changes before running this script"; \
		exit 1; \
	fi

.PHONY: setup-env
setup-env: ensure-branch-clean
	$(PYTHON) tools/nightly.py $(NIGHTLY_TOOL_OPTS)

.PHONY: setup-env-cuda
setup-env-cuda:
	$(MAKE) setup-env PYTHON="$(PYTHON)" NIGHTLY_TOOL_OPTS="$(NIGHTLY_TOOL_OPTS) --cuda"

.PHONY: setup-env-rocm
setup-env-rocm:
	$(MAKE) setup-env PYTHON="$(PYTHON)" NIGHTLY_TOOL_OPTS="$(NIGHTLY_TOOL_OPTS) --rocm"

.PHONY: setup-lint
setup-lint .lintbin/.lintrunner.sha256: requirements.txt pyproject.toml .lintrunner.toml
	@echo "Setting up lintrunner..."
	$(PIP) install lintrunner
	lintrunner init
	@echo "Generating .lintrunner.sha256..."
	@mkdir -p .lintbin
	@sha256sum requirements.txt pyproject.toml .lintrunner.toml > .lintbin/.lintrunner.sha256

.PHONY: lazy-setup-lint
lazy-setup-lint: .lintbin/.lintrunner.sha256
	@if [ ! -x "$(shell command -v lintrunner)" ]; then \
		$(MAKE) setup-lint; \
	fi

.PHONY: lint
lint: lazy-setup-lint
	lintrunner --all-files

.PHONY: quicklint
quicklint: lazy-setup-lint
	lintrunner

.PHONY: quickfix
quickfix: lazy-setup-lint
	lintrunner --apply-patches

# Deprecated target aliases
.PHONY: setup_env setup_env_cuda setup_env_rocm setup_lint
setup_env: setup-env
setup_env_cuda: setup-env-cuda
setup_env_rocm: setup-env-rocm
setup_lint: setup-lint

     4→# You can set these variables from the command line.

    10→PYCMD         ?= python

    12→# Put it first so that "make" without argument is like "make help".

#!/bin/bash
__doc__="
This script simply runs the torch doctests via the xdoctest runner.

This must be run from the root of the torch repo, as it needs the path to the
torch source code.

This script is provided as a developer convenience. On the CI the doctests are
invoked in 'run_test.py'
"
# To simply list tests
# xdoctest -m torch --style=google list

# Reference: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/59895/bash-script-dir
SCRIPT_DIR=$( cd -- "$( dirname -- "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}" )" &> /dev/null && pwd )
TORCH_MODPATH=$SCRIPT_DIR/../torch
echo "TORCH_MODPATH = $TORCH_MODPATH"

if [[ ! -d "$TORCH_MODPATH" ]] ; then
    echo "Could not find the path to the torch module"
else
    export XDOCTEST_GLOBAL_EXEC="from torch import nn\nimport torch.nn.functional as F\nimport torch"
    export XDOCTEST_OPTIONS="+IGNORE_WHITESPACE"
    # Note: google won't catch numpy style docstrings (a few exist) but it also won't fail
    # on things not intended to be doctests.
    export XDOCTEST_STYLE="google"
    xdoctest torch "$TORCH_MODPATH" --style="$XDOCTEST_STYLE" --global-exec "$XDOCTEST_GLOBAL_EXEC" --options="$XDOCTEST_OPTIONS"
fi

#!/bin/bash
__doc__="
This script simply runs the torch doctests via the xdoctest runner.

This must be run from the root of the torch repo, as it needs the path to the
torch source code.

This script is provided as a developer convenience. On the CI the doctests are
invoked in 'run_test.py'
"
# To simply list tests
# xdoctest -m torch --style=google list

# Reference: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/59895/bash-script-dir
SCRIPT_DIR=$( cd -- "$( dirname -- "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}" )" &> /dev/null && pwd )
TORCH_MODPATH=$SCRIPT_DIR/../torch
echo "TORCH_MODPATH = $TORCH_MODPATH"

if [[ ! -d "$TORCH_MODPATH" ]] ; then
    echo "Could not find the path to the torch module"
else
    export XDOCTEST_GLOBAL_EXEC="from torch import nn\nimport torch.nn.functional as F\nimport torch"
    export XDOCTEST_OPTIONS="+IGNORE_WHITESPACE"
    # Note: google won't catch numpy style docstrings (a few exist) but it also won't fail
    # on things not intended to be doctests.
    export XDOCTEST_STYLE="google"
    xdoctest torch "$TORCH_MODPATH" --style="$XDOCTEST_STYLE" --global-exec "$XDOCTEST_GLOBAL_EXEC" --options="$XDOCTEST_OPTIONS"
fi

1→# This makefile does nothing but delegating the actual building to cmake.
     2→
     3→SHELL        = /bin/bash
     4→.SHELLFLAGS := -eu -o pipefail -c
     5→PYTHON      ?= $(shell command -v python3 || command -v python)
     6→PIP          = $(PYTHON) -m pip
     7→NIGHTLY_TOOL_OPTS := pull
     8→
     9→.PHONY: all
    10→all:
    11→	@cmake -S . -B build $(shell $(PYTHON) ./scripts/get_python_cmake_flags.py) && \
    12→		cmake --build build --parallel --
    13→
    14→.PHONY: triton
    15→triton:
    16→	$(PIP) uninstall -y triton
    17→	@./scripts/install_triton_wheel.sh
    18→
    19→.PHONY: clean
    20→clean: # This will remove ALL build folders.
    21→	@rm -r build*/ || true
    22→
    23→.PHONY: linecount
    24→linecount:
    25→	@cloc --read-lang-def=caffe.cloc caffe2 || \
    26→		echo "Cloc is not available on the machine. You can install cloc with " && \
    27→		echo "    sudo apt-get install cloc"
    28→
    29→.PHONY: ensure-branch-clean
    30→ensure-branch-clean:
    31→	@if [ -n "$(shell git status --porcelain)" ]; then \
    32→		echo "Please commit or stash all changes before running this script"; \
    33→		exit 1; \
    34→	fi
    35→
    36→.PHONY: setup-env
    37→setup-env: ensure-branch-clean
    38→	$(PYTHON) tools/nightly.py $(NIGHTLY_TOOL_OPTS)
    39→
    40→.PHONY: setup-env-cuda
    41→setup-env-cuda:
    42→	$(MAKE) setup-env PYTHON="$(PYTHON)" NIGHTLY_TOOL_OPTS="$(NIGHTLY_TOOL_OPTS) --cuda"
    43→
    44→.PHONY: setup-env-rocm
    45→setup-env-rocm:
    46→	$(MAKE) setup-env PYTHON="$(PYTHON)" NIGHTLY_TOOL_OPTS="$(NIGHTLY_TOOL_OPTS) --rocm"
    47→
    48→.PHONY: setup-lint
    49→setup-lint .lintbin/.lintrunner.sha256: requirements.txt pyproject.toml .lintrunner.toml
    50→	@echo "Setting up lintrunner..."
    51→	$(PIP) install lintrunner
    52→	lintrunner init
    53→	@echo "Generating .lintrunner.sha256..."
    54→	@mkdir -p .lintbin
    55→	@sha256sum requirements.txt pyproject.toml .lintrunner.toml > .lintbin/.lintrunner.sha256
    56→
    57→.PHONY: lazy-setup-lint
    58→lazy-setup-lint: .lintbin/.lintrunner.sha256
    59→	@if [ ! -x "$(shell command -v lintrunner)" ]; then \
    60→		$(MAKE) setup-lint; \
    61→	fi
    62→
    63→.PHONY: lint
    64→lint: lazy-setup-lint
    65→	lintrunner --all-files
    66→
    67→.PHONY: quicklint
    68→quicklint: lazy-setup-lint
    69→	lintrunner
    70→
    71→.PHONY: quickfix
    72→quickfix: lazy-setup-lint
    73→	lintrunner --apply-patches
    74→
    75→# Deprecated target aliases
    76→.PHONY: setup_env setup_env_cuda setup_env_rocm setup_lint
    77→setup_env: setup-env
    78→setup_env_cuda: setup-env-cuda
    79→setup_env_rocm: setup-env-rocm
    80→setup_lint: setup-lint

1→```{eval-rst}
     2→.. role:: hidden
     3→    :class: hidden-section
     4→```
     5→
     6→# PyTorch Symmetric Memory
     7→
     8→:::{note}
     9→`torch.distributed._symmetric_memory` is currently in alpha state and under
    10→development. API changes may be possible.
    11→:::
    12→
    13→## Why Symmetric Memory?
    14→
    15→With rapidly evolving parallelization techniques, existing frameworks and
    16→libraries often struggle to keep up, and developers increasingly rely on custom
    17→implementations directly scheduling communications and computations. In recent
    18→years we’ve witnessed a shift from primarily relying on one-dimensional
    19→data-parallelism techniques to multi-dimensional parallelism ones. The latter
    20→have different latency requirements for different types of communications and
    21→thus require fine-grained overlapping of compute and communications.
    22→
    23→To minimize compute interference, they also require the use of copy engines and
    24→network interface cards (NICs) to drive communication. Network transport
    25→protocols such as remote direct memory access (RDMA) enhance the performance by
    26→enabling direct, high-speed, and low-latency communication between processors
    27→and memory. This increase in variety indicates the need for finer-grained
    28→communication primitives than are offered today by high-level collective APIs,
    29→ones that would enable developers to implement specific algorithms tailored for
    30→their use cases, such as low-latency collectives, fine-grained
    31→compute-communications overlap, or custom fusions.
    32→
    33→Furthermore, today’s advanced AI systems connect GPUs with high-bandwidth links
    34→(such as NVLinks, InfiniBand or RoCE), making GPU global memory directly
    35→accessible to peers. Such connections present a great opportunity for
    36→programmers to program the system as a single, gigantic GPU with vast accessible
    37→memory, instead of programming singular “GPU islands.”
    38→
    39→In this document, we will show how you can use PyTorch Symmetric Memory to
    40→program modern GPU systems as a “single GPU” and achieve fine-grained remote
    41→access.
    42→
    43→## What PyTorch Symmetric Memory unlocks?
    44→
    45→PyTorch Symmetric Memory unlocks three new capabilities:
    46→
    47→- **Customized communication patterns**: Increased flexibility in kernel writing
    48→allows developers to write custom kernels that implement their custom
    49→computations and communications, directly tailored to the need of the
    50→application. It will also be straightforward to add support for new data types
    51→along with the special compute that those data types might require, even if it’s
    52→not present yet in the standard libraries.
    53→
    54→- **In-kernel compute-comm fusion**: Device-initiated communication capability
    55→allows developers to write kernels with both computation and communication
    56→instructions, allowing for the fusion of computation and data movement in the
    57→smallest possible granularity.
    58→
    59→- **Low-latency remote access**: Network transport protocols like RDMA enhance the
    60→performance of symmetric memory in networked environments by enabling direct,
    61→high-speed, and low-latency communication between processors and memory. RDMA
    62→eliminates the overhead associated with the traditional network stack and CPU
    63→involvement. It also offloads data transfer from the compute to the NICs,
    64→freeing up compute resources for computational tasks.
    65→
    66→Next, we will show you how PyTorch Symmetric Memory (SymmMem) enables new
    67→applications with the above capabilities.
    68→
    69→## A “Hello World” example
    70→
    71→The PyTorch SymmMem programming model involves two key elements:
    72→
    73→- creating symmetric tensors
    74→- creating SymmMem kernels
    75→
    76→To create symmetric tensors, one can use the
    77→`torch.distributed._symmetric_memory` package:
    78→
    79→```python
    80→import torch.distributed._symmetric_memory as symm_mem
    81→
    82→t = symm_mem.empty(128, device=torch.device("cuda", rank))
    83→hdl = symm_mem.rendezvous(t, group)
    84→```
    85→
    86→The `symm_mem.empty` function creates a tensor that is backed by a symmetric
    87→memory allocation. The `rendezvous` function establishes a rendezvous with peers
    88→in the group, and returns a handle to the symmetric memory allocation. The
    89→handle provides method to access information related to the symmetric memory
    90→allocation, such as pointers to symmetric buffer on peer ranks, multicast
    91→pointer (if supported), and signal pads.
    92→
    93→The `empty` and `rendezvous` functions must be called in the same order on all
    94→ranks in the group.
    95→
    96→Then, collectives can be called on these tensors. For example, to perform a
    97→one-shot all-reduce:
    98→
    99→```python
   100→# Most SymmMem ops are under the torch.ops.symm_mem namespace
   101→torch.ops.symm_mem.one_shot_all_reduce(t, "sum", group)
   102→```
   103→
   104→Please note that `torch.ops.symm_mem` is an "op namespace" instead of a python
   105→module. Therefore, you can't import it by `import torch.ops.symm_mem`, neither
   106→can you import an op by `from torch.ops.symm_mem import one_shot_all_reduce`.
   107→You can call the op directly as in the example above.
   108→
   109→## Write your own kernel
   110→
   111→To write your own kernel doing communications with symmetric memory, you’ll need
   112→access to the addresses of mapped peer buffers and access to signal pads that
   113→are required for synchronization. In the kernel you’ll also need to perform
   114→correct synchronizations to make sure that peers are ready for communication,
   115→and signal to them that this GPU is ready.
   116→
   117→PyTorch Symmetric Memory provides CUDA Graph-compatible synchronization
   118→primitives that operate on the signal pad accompanying each symmetric memory
   119→allocation. Kernels using symmetric memory can be written both in CUDA and in
   120→Triton. Here’s an example allocating symmetric tensor and exchanging handles:
   121→
   122→```python
   123→import torch.distributed._symmetric_memory as symm_mem
   124→
   125→dist.init_process_group()
   126→rank = dist.get_rank()
   127→
   128→# Allocate a tensor
   129→t = symm_mem.empty(4096, device=f"cuda:{rank}")
   130→# Establish symmetric memory and obtain the handle
   131→hdl = symm_mem.rendezvous(t, dist.group.WORLD)
   132→```
   133→
   134→Access to buffer pointers, multimem pointer, and signal pads is provided via:
   135→
   136→```python
   137→hdl.buffer_ptrs
   138→hdl.multicast_ptr
   139→hdl.signal_pad_ptrs
   140→```
   141→
   142→Data pointed to by `buffer_ptrs` can be accessed just like regular local data,
   143→and any necessary compute can also be performed in the usual ways. As with local
   144→data, you can and should use vectorized accesses to improve efficiency.
   145→
   146→Symmetric memory is especially convenient for writing kernels in Triton. While
   147→previously Triton removed the barriers to writing efficient CUDA code, now
   148→communications can be added easily to Triton kernels. The kernel below
   149→demonstrates a low-latency, all-reduce kernel written in Triton.
   150→
   151→```python
   152→@triton.jit
   153→def one_shot_all_reduce_kernel(
   154→    buf_tuple,
   155→    signal_pad_ptrs,
   156→    output_ptr,
   157→    numel: tl.constexpr,
   158→    rank: tl.constexpr,
   159→    world_size: tl.constexpr,
   160→    BLOCK_SIZE: tl.constexpr,
   161→):
   162→    ptx_utils.symm_mem_sync(
   163→        signal_pad_ptrs, None, rank, world_size, hasSubsequenceMemAccess=True
   164→    )
   165→
   166→    pid = tl.program_id(axis=0)
   167→    block_start = pid * BLOCK_SIZE
   168→
   169→    while block_start < numel:
   170→        offsets = block_start + tl.arange(0, BLOCK_SIZE)
   171→        mask = offsets < numel
   172→        acc = tl.zeros((BLOCK_SIZE,), dtype=tl.bfloat16)
   173→
   174→        for i in tl.static_range(world_size):
   175→            buffer_rank = buf_tuple[i]
   176→            x = tl.load(buffer_rank + offsets, mask=mask)
   177→            acc += x
   178→
   179→        tl.store(output_ptr + offsets, acc, mask=mask)
   180→        block_start += tl.num_programs(axis=0) * BLOCK_SIZE
   181→
   182→    ptx_utils.symm_mem_sync(
   183→        signal_pad_ptrs, None, rank, world_size, hasPreviousMemAccess=True
   184→    )
   185→```
   186→
   187→Synchronizations at the beginning and the end of the kernel above guarantee that
   188→all the processes see consistent data. The bulk of the kernel is recognizable
   189→Triton code, and Triton will optimize it behind the scene, making sure memory
   190→accesses are performed in an efficient way with vectorization and unrolling. As
   191→with all Triton kernels, it is easily modifiable to add extra computations or
   192→change the communication algorithm. Visit
   193→https://github.com/meta-pytorch/kraken/blob/main/kraken to see additional
   194→utilities and examples of using symmetric memory to implement common patterns in
   195→Triton.
   196→
   197→## Scale out
   198→
   199→Large language models distribute experts onto more than 8 GPUs, hence requiring
   200→multi-node access capability. NICs capable of RDMA come to help. In addition,
   201→software libraries such as NVSHMEM or rocSHMEM abstract away the programming
   202→difference between intra-node access and inter-node access with primitives that
   203→are slightly higher level than pointer access, such as put and get.
   204→
   205→PyTorch provides NVSHMEM plugins to augment Triton kernels’ cross-node
   206→capabilities. As shown in the code snippet below, one can initiate a cross-node
   207→put command within the kernel.
   208→
   209→```python
   210→import torch.distributed._symmetric_memory._nvshmem_triton as nvshmem
   211→from torch.distributed._symmetric_memory._nvshmem_triton import requires_nvshmem
   212→
   213→@requires_nvshmem
   214→@triton.jit
   215→def my_put_kernel(
   216→    dest,
   217→    src,
   218→    nelems,
   219→    pe,
   220→):
   221→    nvshmem.put(dest, src, nelems, pe)
   222→```
   223→
   224→The `requires_nvshmem` decorator is used to indicate that the kernel requires
   225→the NVSHMEM device library as an external dependency. When Triton compiles the
   226→kernel, the decorator will search your system paths for the NVSHMEM device
   227→library. If it is available, Triton will include the necessary device assembly
   228→to use the NVSHMEM functions.
   229→
   230→## API Reference
   231→
   232→```{eval-rst}
   233→.. currentmodule:: torch.distributed._symmetric_memory
   234→```

   238→```
   239→

   254→```
   255→
   256→## Op Reference
   257→:::{note}
   258→The following ops are hosted in the `torch.ops.symm_mem` namespace. You can call
   259→them directly via `torch.ops.symm_mem.<op_name>`.
   260→:::
   261→
   262→```{eval-rst}
   263→.. currentmodule:: torch.ops.symm_mem
   264→```
   265→
   266→```{eval-rst}
   267→.. py:function:: multimem_all_reduce_(input: Tensor, reduce_op: str, group_name: str) -> Tensor
   268→
   269→    Performs a multimem all-reduce operation on the input tensor. This operation
   270→    requires hardware support for multimem operations. On NVIDIA GPUs, NVLink
   271→    SHARP is required.
   272→
   273→    :param Tensor input: Input tensor to perform all-reduce on. Must be symmetric.
   274→    :param str reduce_op: Reduction operation to perform. Currently only "sum" is supported.
   275→    :param str group_name: Name of the group to perform all-reduce on.
   276→
   277→
   278→.. py:function:: multimem_all_gather_out(input: Tensor, group_name: str, out: Tensor) -> Tensor
   279→
   280→    Performs a multimem all-gather operation on the input tensor. This operation requires hardware support for multimem operations. On NVIDIA GPUs, NVLink SHARP is required.
   281→
   282→    :param Tensor input: Input tensor to perform all-gather on.
   283→    :param str group_name: Name of the group to perform all-gather on.
   284→    :param Tensor out: Output tensor to store the result of the all-gather operation. Must be symmetric.
   285→
   286→
   287→.. py:function:: one_shot_all_reduce(input: Tensor, reduce_op: str, group_name: str) -> Tensor
   288→
   289→    Performs a one-shot all-reduce operation on the input tensor.
   290→

   292→    :param str reduce_op: Reduction operation to perform. Currently only "sum" is supported.
   293→    :param str group_name: Name of the group to perform all-reduce on.
   294→
   295→
   296→.. py:function:: one_shot_all_reduce_out(input: Tensor, reduce_op: str, group_name: str, out: Tensor) -> Tensor
   297→
   298→    Performs a one-shot all-reduce operation based on the input tensor and writes the result to the output tensor.
   299→
   300→    :param Tensor input: Input tensor to perform all-reduce on. Must be symmetric.
   301→    :param str reduce_op: Reduction operation to perform. Currently only "sum" is supported.
   302→    :param str group_name: Name of the group to perform all-reduce on.
   303→    :param Tensor out: Output tensor to store the result of the all-reduce operation. Can be a regular tensor.
   304→
   305→
   306→.. py:function:: two_shot_all_reduce_(input: Tensor, reduce_op: str, group_name: str) -> Tensor
   307→
   308→    Performs a two-shot all-reduce operation on the input tensor.
   309→
   310→    :param Tensor input: Input tensor to perform all-reduce on. Must be symmetric.
   311→    :param str reduce_op: Reduction operation to perform. Currently only "sum" is supported.
   312→    :param str group_name: Name of the group to perform all-reduce on.
   313→
   314→
   315→.. py:function:: all_to_all_vdev(input: Tensor, out: Tensor, in_splits: Tensor, out_splits_offsets: Tensor, group_name: str) -> None
   316→
   317→    Performs an all-to-all-v operation using NVSHMEM, with split information provided on device.
   318→
   319→    :param Tensor input: Input tensor to perform all-to-all on. Must be symmetric.
   320→    :param Tensor out: Output tensor to store the result of the all-to-all operation. Must be symmetric.
   321→    :param Tensor in_splits: Tensor containing splits of data to send to each peer. Must be symmetric. Must be of size (group_size,). The splits are in the unit of elements in the 1st dimension.
   322→    :param Tensor out_splits_offsets: Tensor containing the splits and offsets of data received from each peer. Must be symmetric. Must be of size (2, group_size). The rows are (in order): output splits and output offsets.
   323→    :param str group_name: Name of the group to perform all-to-all on.
   324→
   325→
   326→.. py:function:: all_to_all_vdev_2d(input: Tensor, out: Tensor, in_splits: Tensor, out_splits_offsets: Tensor, group_name: str, [major_align: int = None]) -> None
   327→
   328→    Perform a 2D all-to-all-v operation using NVSHMEM, with split information provided on device. In Mixture of Experts models, this operation can be used to dispatch tokens.
   329→
   330→    :param Tensor input: Input tensor to perform all-to-all on. Must be symmetric.
   331→    :param Tensor out: Output tensor to store the result of the all-to-all operation. Must be symmetric.
   332→    :param Tensor in_splits: Tensor containing the splits of data to send to each expert. Must be symmetric. Must be of size (group_size * ne,), where ne is the number of experts per rank. The splits are in the unit of elements in the 1st dimension.
   333→    :param Tensor out_splits_offsets: Tensor containing the splits and offsets of data received from each peer. Must be symmetric. Must be of size (2, group_size * ne). The rows are (in order): output splits and output offsets.
   334→    :param str group_name: Name of the group to perform all-to-all on.
   335→    :param int major_align: Optional alignment for the major dimension of the output chunk for each expert. If not provided, the alignment is assumed to be 1. Any alignment adjustment will be reflected in the output offsets.
   336→
   337→    A 2D AllToAllv shuffle is illustrated below:
   338→    (world_size = 2, ne = 2, total number of experts = 4)::
   339→
   340→      Source: |       Rank 0      |       Rank 1      |
   341→              | c0 | c1 | c2 | c3 | d0 | d1 | d2 | d3 |
   342→
   343→      Dest  : |       Rank 0      |       Rank 1      |
   344→              | c0 | d0 | c1 | d1 | c2 | d2 | c3 | d3 |
   345→
   346→    where each `c_i` / `d_i` are slices of the `input` tensor, targeting expert
   347→    `i`, with length indicated by input splits.  That is, the 2D AllToAllv
   348→    shuffle achieves a transpose from rank-major order at input to expert-major
   349→    order at output.
   350→
   351→    If `major_align` is not 1, the output offsets of c1, c2, c3 will be
   352→    up-aligned to this value. For example, if c0 has length 5 and d0 has
   353→    length 7 (making a total of 12), and if the `major_align` is set to 16,
   354→    the output offset of c1 will be 16. Similar for c2 and c3. This value has
   355→    no effect on the offset of the minor dimension, i.e.  d0, d1, d2 and d3.
   356→    Note: since cutlass does not support empty bins, we set the aligned length
   357→    to `major_align` if it is 0. See
   358→    https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/152668.
   359→
   360→
   361→.. py:function:: all_to_all_vdev_2d_offset(Tensor input, Tensor out, Tensor in_splits_offsets, Tensor out_splits_offsets, str group_name) -> None
   362→
   363→    Perform a 2D AllToAllv shuffle operation, with input split and offset
   364→    information provided on device. The input offsets are not required to be
   365→    exact prefix sum of the input splits, i.e. paddings are allowed between the
   366→    split chunks. The paddings, however, will not be transferred to peer
   367→    ranks.
   368→
   369→    In Mixture of Experts models, this operation can be used to combine tokens
   370→    processed by experts on parallel ranks. This operation can be viewed as an
   371→    "reverse" operation to the `all_to_all_vdev_2d` operation (which shuffles
   372→    tokens to experts).
   373→
   374→    :param Tensor input: Input tensor to perform all-to-all on. Must be symmetric.
   375→    :param Tensor out: Output tensor to store the result of the all-to-all operation. Must be symmetric.
   376→    :param Tensor in_splits_offsets: Tensor containing the splits and offsets of data to send to each expert. Must be symmetric. Must be of size (2, group_size * ne), where `ne` is the number of experts. The rows are (in order): input splits and input offsets. The splits are in the unit of elements in the 1st dimension.
   377→    :param Tensor out_splits_offsets: Tensor containing the splits and offsets of data received from each peer. Must be symmetric. Must be of size (2, group_size * ne). The rows are (in order): output splits and output offsets.
